relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The crossing is not complete until the whole people is across.
Each tribe appoints one carrier of memory.
Stones are lifted from the place where priests stood inside the river.
Joshua gathers the twelve representatives.
Each shoulder bears one stone for one tribe.
The memorial is designed to create a child's question.
The answer remembers water interrupted before covenant presence.
The people carry the river's testimony into their camp.
Another memory is placed beneath the waterline where priests stood.
Sacred service remains in the river until every instruction and every crossing is complete.
Only after the people reach safety do ark and priests emerge.
The eastern tribes keep their promise and cross armed.
Forty thousand enter the plains prepared for war.
Joshua's public stature rises beside Moses' memory.
YHWH speaks the moment of release.
The priests are called out from the river.
Joshua passes the command onward.
As the last feet reach land, the Jordan returns to flood.
The people camp at Gilgal on the tenth day of the first month.
Twelve river stones become a visible memory at the new camp.
Again the future is imagined as a child asking what the stones mean.
The answer is a story of a people crossing on dry ground.
Jordan is joined to the Sea of Reeds in one remembered pattern.
The sign is meant to reveal power and cultivate reverence among all peoples.

contemplative_note: Joshua 4 understands that memory should invite questions. A faithful monument does not command admiration; it gives the next generation enough truth to ask what happened here—and whose lives the story includes or leaves out.
